source: Mit lÃ¤chelndem Gesichte schaute Siddhartha dem hinweggehenden MÃ¶nche nach. Der Schlaf hatte ihn sehr gestÃ¤rkt, sehr aber quÃ¤lte ihn der Hunger, denn er hatte nun zwei Tage nichts gegessen, und lange war die Zeit vorÃ¼ber, da er hart gegen den Hunger gewesen war. Mit Kummer, und doch auch mit Lachen, gedachte er jener Zeit. Damals, so erinnerte er sich, hatte er sich vor Kamala dreier Dinge gerÃ¼hmt, hatte drei edle und unÃ¼berwindliche KÃ¼nste gekonnt: Fasten â?Warten â?Denken. Dies war sein Besitz gewesen, seine Macht und Kraft, sein fester Stab, in den fleiÃigen, mÃ¼hseligen Jahren seiner Jugend hatte er diese drei KÃ¼nste gelernt, nichts anderes. Und nun hatten sie ihn verlassen, keine von ihnen war mehr sein, nicht Fasten, nicht Warten, nicht Denken. Um das Elendeste hatte er sie hingegeben, um das VergÃ¤nglichste, um SinnenIust, um Wohlleben, um Reichtum! Seltsam war es ihm in der Tat ergangen. Und jetzt, so schien es, jetzt war er wirklich ein Kindermensch geworden.
translation_a: With a smiling face, Siddhartha watched the departing monk. Sleep had greatly strengthened him, but hunger tormented him greatly, for he had not eaten for two days, and the time was long gone when he had been hard against hunger. With sorrow, and yet also with laughter, he thought of that time. At that time, he recalled, he had boasted three things to Kamala, had mastered three noble and insurmountable arts: Fasting - Waiting - Thinking. This had been his possession, his might and strength, his steady staff, in the busy, toilsome years of his youth he had learned these three arts, nothing else. And now they had left him, none of them was his anymore, not fasting, not waiting, not thinking. He had given her up for what was most miserable, for what was most perishable, for sensual pleasure, for a good life, for riches! It had indeed been strange for him. And now, it seemed, he really had become a childish man.
translation_b: With a smiling face, Siddhartha peered after the departing monk. Sleep had greatly strengthened him, but hunger now greatly tormented him, for he had not eaten in two days, and long past was the time when he had been hard toward hunger. With grief, and yet with laughter too, he thought back to that time. He recalled that he had boasted of three things to Kamala; he had been master of three noble and invincible arts: fastingâwaitingâthinking. That had been his property, his might and strength, his solid staff. In the zealous, arduous years of his youth he had learned those three arts, and nothing else. And now they had abandoned him; none of them were his anymore: not fasting, not waiting, not thinking. He had given them away for the most wretched things, the most ephemeral things, for sensuality, for luxury, for wealth! His life had indeed been bizarre. And now, so it seemed, he had truly become a child person.
comment: The first translation is too literal and doesn't quite show understanding of the original ("had become a childish man" - that is not what the original intended to say, the second translation gets this better). The second translation uses better words overall and also better expressions - "His life had indeed been bizarre" is a more accurate and also much better-sounding sentence than "It had indeed been strange for him." The second translation features a better mastery of language and a better representation of the original.

source: PlÃ¶tzlich zuckte Hans Castorp geÃ¤rgert und beleidigt zusammen. Eine TÃ¼r war zugefallen, es war die TÃ¼r links vorn, die gleich in die Halle fÃ¼hrte, â?jemand hatte sie zufallen lassen oder gar hinter sich ins SchloÃ geworfen, und das war ein GerÃ¤usch, das Hans Castorp auf den Tod nicht leiden konnte, das er von jeher gehaÃt hatte. Vielleicht beruhte dieser HaÃ auf Erziehung, vielleicht auf angeborener Idiosynkrasie, â?genug, er verabscheute das TÃ¼renwerfen und hÃ¤tte jeden schlagen kÃ¶nnen, der es sich vor seinen Ohren zuschulden kommen lieÃ. In diesem Fall war die TÃ¼r obendrein mit kleinen Glasscheiben gefÃ¼llt, und das verstÃ¤rkte den Chok: es war ein Schmettern und Klirren. Pfui, dachte Hans Castorp wÃ¼tend, was ist denn das fÃ¼r eine verdammte Schlamperei! Da Ã¼brigens in demselben Augenblick die NÃ¤hterin das Wort an ihn richtete, so hatte er keine Zeit, festzustellen, wer der MissetÃ¤ter gewesen sei. Doch standen Falten zwischen seinen blonden Brauen, und sein Gesicht war peinlich verzerrt, wÃ¤hrend er der NÃ¤hterin antwortete.
translation_a: Suddenly Hans Castorp winced, annoyed and offended. A door had slammed shut, it was the front left door that led straight into the hall - someone had let it shut or even slammed it behind them, and that was a noise that Hans Castorp couldn't stand, that he had always hated. Perhaps that hatred was from education, perhaps from innate idiosyncrasyâenough he detested door-smashing, and could have punched anyone who threw it in front of his ears. In this case the door was also filled with small panes of glass, and that added to the shock: it was a smash and clink. Pfui, thought Hans Castorp angrily, what kind of damned sloppiness is that! Incidentally, since the seamstress spoke to him at the same moment, he had no time to establish who the culprit was. But there were lines between his blond brows and his face was awkwardly contorted as he answered the seamstress.
translation_b: Suddenly Hans Castorp flinchedâhe was annoyed and offended. A door, the one to his left that led to the lobby, had banged shutâsomeone had simply let it slam, or perhaps even slammed it intentionally, and that was a noise that Hans Castorp absolutely could not tolerate, he had always hated it. Perhaps it was a learned dislike, perhaps an inborn idiosyncrasyâ?whatever it was, he abhorred banging doors and could have slapped anyone guilty of slamming one within earshot. In this case, the door was divided into little glass panes, which only heightened the shock: it was a bang and a rattle. âDamn it,â?Hans Castorp thought angrily, âwhat kind of sloppiness is that?â?Since the seamstress had said something to him at the same moment, he had no chance to determine who the malefactor was. But as he answered the seamstress, there were deep furrows between his blond eyebrows and his face was wrenched with distress.
comment: The first translation uses less good sentence structure, some parts sound a bit awkward. It also for some reason still contains the German word "Pfui" instead of its translation. The second translation sounds much smoother, it uses better sentence structure and also better words and expressions (for example "within earshot" vs. "in front of his ears". While the first translation tends to be too literal in places, the second focuses on the meaning and sounds more natural and sophisticated.

source: Vorgestern kam der Medikus hier aus der Stadt hinaus zum Amtmann und fand mich auf der Erde unter Lottens Kindern, wie einige auf mir herumkrabbelten, andere mich neckten, und wie ich sie kitzelte und ein groÃes Geschrei mit ihnen erregte. Der Doktor, der eine sehr dogmatische Drahtpuppe ist, unterm Reden seine Manschetten in Falten legt und einen KrÃ¤usel ohne Ende herauszupft, fand dieses unter der WÃ¼rde eines gescheiten Menschen; das merkte ich an seiner Nase. Ich lieÃ mich aber in nichts stÃ¶ren, lieÃ ihn sehr vernÃ¼nftige Sachen abhandeln und baute den Kindern ihre KartenhÃ¤user wieder, die sie zerschlagen hatten. Auch ging er darauf in der Stadt herum und beklagte, des Amtmanns Kinder wÃ¤ren so schon ungezogen genug, der Werther verderbe sie nun vÃ¶llig.
translation_a: The day before yesterday the medicus came out of town to the bailiff and found me on the ground among Lotte's children, some crawling on me, others teasing me, and how I tickled them and excited a great hooting with them. The doctor, who is a very dogmatic wire doll, who folds his cuffs while he talks and pulls out a ruffle without end, found this beneath the dignity of a clever person; I could tell by his nose. But I didn't let myself be disturbed, I let him deal with very sensible things and rebuilt the children's houses of cards that they had smashed. After that he went around town and complained that the bailiff's children were already naughty enough that Werther was now spoiling them completely.
translation_b: The day before yesterday the doctor from our town came out to the lodge and found me on the floor with several of Lotteâs children on top of me, the rest teasing me. He saw me tickling them and succeeding generally in creating an uproar. The doctor is a dogmatic puppet, constantly repleating his cuffs as he talks and pulling out a loose thread here and there. Of course, he found my behavior undignified for a man of my intellect. I could tell by the way he turned up his nose at the whole thing. I didnât let it bother me, but as he went about his more sensible business, I rebuilt the childrenâs house of cards, which they had toppled; whereupon he went about town telling everyone that the magistrateâs children had always been wild, but now Werther was ruining them completely.
comment: While the first translation starts out smoother, the second translation used much better language overall. It captures the sense of the original better whereas the first translation is often too literal. The wording in the second translation sounds more appropriate (for example "behavior undignified for a man of my intellect" or "I didn't let it bother me" in comparison to the expressions used in the first translation). The last sentence of the first translation also contains an error - there should be a "now" or "and" before "that Werther was now spoiling them completely", though the term "spoiling" is better than the second translations "ruining".

source: Und von Mal zu Mal ward sein LÃ¤cheln dem des FÃ¤hrmanns Ã¤hnlicher, ward beinahe ebenso strahlend, beinahe ebenso von GlÃ¼ck durchglÃ¤nzt, ebenso aus tausend kleinen Falten leuchtend, ebenso kindlich, ebenso greisenhaft. Viele Reisende, wenn sie die beiden FÃ¤hrmÃ¤nner sahen, hielten sie fÃ¼r BrÃ¼der. Oft saÃen sie am Abend gemeinsam beim Ufer auf dem Baumstamm, schwiegen und hÃ¶rten beide dem Wasser zu, welches fÃ¼r sie kein Wasser war, sondern die Stimme des Lebens, die Stimme des Seienden, des ewig Werdenden. Und es geschah zuweilen, dass beide beim AnhÃ¶ren des Flusses an dieselben Dinge dachten, an ein GesprÃ¤ch von vorgestern, an einen ihrer Reisenden, dessen Gesicht und Schicksal sie beschÃ¤ftigte, an den Tod, an ihre Kindheit, und dass sie beide im selben Augenblick, wenn der Fluss ihnen etwas Gutes gesagt hatte, einander anblickten, beide genau dasselbe denkend, beide beglÃ¼ckt Ã¼ber dieselbe Antwort auf dieselbe Frage.
translation_a: And little by little, his smile grew more similar to the ferrymanâs, became almost as radiant, almost as blissful, likewise shining from a thousand tiny creases, likewise a youngsterâs, likewise an oldsterâs. Many travelers who saw the two ferrymen thought they were brothers. On many evenings, they sat together at the tree trunk by the bank, silently listening to the water, which was no water for them, but the voice of life, the voice of Being, the voice of eternal Becoming. And there were moments when both, while hearing the river, thought of the same things, of a conversation from two days ago, of one of their passengers whose face and fate occupied their minds, of death, of their childhoods, and both of them in the same moment, when the river had said something good to them, looked at each other, both thinking the exact same thoughts, both blissful at this same answer to the same question.
translation_b: And from time to time his smile became more like that of the ferryman, became almost as radiant, almost as radiant with happiness, just as radiant with a thousand small wrinkles, just as childlike, just as senile. Many travelers, when they saw the two ferrymen, took them for brothers. In the evening they often sat together on the tree trunk by the bank, were silent and both listened to the water, which for them was not water, but the voice of life, the voice of being, of eternal becoming. And it sometimes happened that while listening to the river both thought of the same things, of a conversation from the day before yesterday, of one of their travelers, whose face and fate occupied them, of death, of their childhood, and that they both at the same moment, when the river had said something good to them, looked at each other, both thinking exactly the same thing, both happy with the same answer to the same question.
comment: Neither translation is good, both contain mistakes. The second one uses radiant three times in a row, neglecting the fact that three different words were used in the German version. This something the first translation manages better. The first translation gets a preposition wrong ("at the tree trunk") and the structure of the last sentence is awkward, still it handles some things better that the second translation does wrong (such as the "radiant" and the use of the words "senile", which is wrong in that context). So the first translation is marginally better than the second one.

source: Das Wort brauchte einige Zeit, bis es ihm ins BewuÃtsein drang. Dann fuhr er auf, wirr um sich blickend, wie ein aus dem Schlaf GestÃ¶rter. Ihr GesprÃ¤ch war ziemlich langsam vonstatten gegangen, da Hans Castorp das FranzÃ¶sische schwerfÃ¤llig und wie in zÃ¶gerndem Sinnen sprach. Das Klavier, das kurze Zeit {512}geschwiegen hatte, tÃ¶nte wieder, nunmehr unter den HÃ¤nden des Mannheimers, der den SlawenjÃ¼ngling abgelÃ¶st und Noten aufgelegt hatte. FrÃ¤ulein Engelhart saÃ bei ihm und blÃ¤tterte um. Der Ball hatte sich gelichtet. Eine grÃ¶Ãere Anzahl der PensionÃ¤re schien horizontale Lage eingenommen zu haben. Vor ihnen saÃ niemand mehr. Im Lesezimmer spielte man Karten.
translation_a: It took a while before what she had said penetrated his consciousness. But then he started up, looking about in befuddlement, like someone rudely awakened from sleep. Their conversation had proceeded rather slowly, because Hans Castorpâs French was clumsy and he spoke haltingly as he tried to express himself. The piano, which had briefly fallen silent, struck up again, now under the hands of the man from Mannheim, who had taken over for the Slavic lad. He had spread his music out before him, and FrÃ¤ulein Engelhart now sat down next to him to turn pages. The party was thinning out. The majority of the residents appeared to have assumed the horizontal position. There was no one sitting in front of them now. People were again playing cards in the reading room.
translation_b: It took a while for the word to sink in. Then he started up, looking around confusedly, like someone disturbed from his sleep. Their conversation had been rather slow, as Hans Castorp spoke French heavily and as if hesitantly. The piano, which had been silent for a short time, sounded again, now in the hands of the man from Mannheim, who had relieved the Slav youth and put on music. Fraulein Engelhart sat with him and turned the pages. The ball had cleared. A larger number of pensioners seemed to have assumed a horizontal position. Nobody was sitting in front of them anymore. They played cards in the reading room.
comment: While the second translation starts off much better, smoother, it then becomes more awkward in wording. The first translation veers awar from the to literal and thereby avoids bad translations like "pensioners" instead of "residents". Also, the last sentence is translated in much better style in the first translation, a good example of avoiding the too literal when necessary.

source: So ging es mit Wiedemann und Sonnenschein. Alle, die es sahen, bebten noch stundenlang. Es ist vergleichsweise eine Wohltat, im Gegensatz zu solcher Misere von einem wahren Ehrenhandel zu erzÃ¤hlen, der ebenfalls dieser Periode angehÃ¶rt, und der seinen Namen allerdings, der formalen Feierlichkeit wegen, mit der er gehandhabt wurde, bis zur LÃ¤cherlichkeit verdiente. Hans Castorp wohnte ihm in seinen einzelnen Phasen nicht bei, sondern belehrte sich Ã¼ber den verwickelten und dramatischen Hergang nur an der Hand von Dokumenten, ErklÃ¤rungen und Protokollen, die, diese Sache betreffend, im Hause Berghof und auÃerhalb seiner, nÃ¤mlich nicht nur am Ort, im Kanton, im Lande, sondern auch im Auslande und in Amerika abschriftlich vertrieben und auch solchen zum Studium zugestellt wurden, von denen ohne weiteres sicher sein muÃte, daÃ sie der Angelegenheit auch nicht einen Deut von Teilnahme widmen konnten und wollten.
translation_a: That was the Wiedemann-Sonnenschein affair. Everyone who witnessed it was still quivering hours later. In contrast to such misery, it is a comparative pleasure to tell about a genuine âaffair of honorâ?that belongs to this same period and that definitely deserves the name, absurdly so, given the solemn formality with which it was carried out. Hans Castorp was not present at its various phases, but only learned about the complicated and dramatic course of events from documents, affidavits, and official minutes devoted to the affair, copies of which were circulated not only in the Berghof, not only in Davos, in the canton of GraubÃ¼nden, in Switzerland, but also abroad, were sent as far as America, and were made available for study to persons who, one can be sure, would not and could not pay one whit of attention to the matter.
translation_b: That's how it went with Wiedemann and sunshine. All who saw it continued to tremble for hours. It is comparatively a blessing, in contrast to such misery, to tell of a true bargain of honour, which also belongs to this period and which certainly deserved its name to the point of ridicule because of the formal solemnity with which it was handled. Hans Castorp did not attend its individual phases, but informed himself about the complicated and dramatic course of events only by means of documents, declarations and protocols, which, concerning this matter, were kept in the Berghof house and outside of him, namely not only at the place copies were distributed in the canton, in the country, but also abroad and in America, and were also sent to those for study who had to be sure that they could not and did not want to devote the least bit of interest to the matter.
comment: The second translation is awkwardly worded and contains some mistakes. It is also too literal. The first translation reads smoother, has better wording and focuses on the sense, not the literal translation.

source: Am wieder nÃ¤chsten Tage jedoch trat eine Erscheinung auf, die Hans Castorp bestimmte, sich innerlich von der Form zu trennen und zu lÃ¶sen und tatsÃ¤chlich dem Professionisten, dem Ã¼blen HÃ¼ter der PietÃ¤t, das Feld zu Ã¼berlassen. Joachim nÃ¤mlich, dessen Ausdruck bisher so ernst und ehrbar gewesen, hatte in seinem Kriegerbarte zu lÃ¤cheln begonnen, und Hans Castorp verhehlte sich nicht, daÃ dieses LÃ¤cheln die Neigung zur Ausartung in sich trug â?es erfÃ¼llte sein Herz mit Empfindungen der Eile. So war es in Gottes Namen denn gut, daÃ die Abholung bevorstand, der Sarg geschlossen und verschraubt werden sollte. Hans Castorp berÃ¼hrte, eingeborene SittensprÃ¶digkeit beiseite setzend, seines ehemaligen Joachim steinkalte Stirn zum Abschied zart mit den Lippen und ging trotz allem MiÃtrauen gegen den Mann der Kehrseite mit Luise ZiemÃen folgsam zum Zimmer hinaus.
translation_a: The very next day, however, an apparition appeared that determined Hans Castorp to separate himself inwardly from the form and actually leave the field to the professional, the evil guardian of piety. Joachim, whose expression had hitherto been so serious and honorable, had begun to smile in his warrior's beard, and Hans Castorp made no secret of the fact that this smile contained a tendency to degeneracy - it filled his heart with feelings of haste. So it was good in God's name that the collection was imminent, the coffin was to be closed and bolted. Hans Castorp, setting aside native moral primness, gently touched his former Joachim's stone-cold forehead with his lips in farewell and, in spite of all mistrust of the man on the other side, obediently went out of the room with Luise Ziemssen.
translation_b: Two days later, however, something occurred to convince Hans Castorp to detach himself emotionally from this shell and leave things to the professional, that offensive guardian of piety. Joachim, whose expression so far had been earnest and honorable, began to smile under his beard. Hans Castorp could not help admitting that this smile bore within it the seeds of degenerationâand it filled his heart with a sense of haste. Thank God, the body would soon be picked up, the coffin closed and screwed shut. Setting aside his native reserve, Hans Castorp touched his lips in a gesture of tender farewell to the stone-cold brow of what had once been Joachim, and despite his misgivings, turned his back on the undertaker and meekly followed Luise Ziemssen out of the room.
comment: The second translation captures the sense of the text better, whereas the first translation is too literal in places. In translation one it for example says "Hans Castorp made no secret of the fact" which is not a correct translation as Hans Castop admits it to himself - the second translation handles it correctly ("Hans Castorp could not help admitting"). Translation 2 makes relevant small changes in wording to provide the actual meaning of the text.

source: Jâai promis un dialogue, je tiens parole. â?CâÃ©tait le matin Ã lâaube du jour : les rayons du soleil doraient Ã la fois le sommet du mont Viso et celui des montagnes les plus Ã©levÃ©es de lâÃ®le qui est Ã nos antipodes ; et dÃ©jÃ elle Ã©tait Ã©veillÃ©e, soit que son rÃ©veil prÃ©maturÃ© fÃ»t lâeffet des visions nocturnes qui la mettent souvent dans une agitation aussi fatigante quâinutile, soit que le carnaval, qui tirait alors vers sa fin, fÃ»t la cause occulte de son rÃ©veil, ce temps de plaisir et de folie ayant une influence sur la machine humaine comme les phases de la lune et de la conjonction de certaines planÃ¨tes. â?Enfin, elle Ã©tait Ã©veillÃ©e et trÃ¨s Ã©veillÃ©e, lorsque mon Ã¢me se dÃ©barrassa elle-mÃªme des liens du sommeil.
translation_a: I promised a dialogue, I keep my word. â?It was morning at daybreak: the rays of the sun gilded both the summit of Mount Viso and that of the highest mountains of the island which is at our antipodes; and she was already awake, either because her premature awakening was the effect of the nocturnal visions which often put her in an agitation as tiring as it was useless, or because the carnival, which was then drawing towards its end, was the occult cause of her awakening. , this time of pleasure and madness having an influence on the human machine like the phases of the moon and the conjunction of certain planets. âFinally, she was awake, and wide awake, when my soul rid itself of the bonds of sleep.
translation_b: I promised a dialogue, and I will keep my word.âOne morning, at daybreak, as the sunâs rays were gilding the peaks of Mount Viso and of the highest mountains of the island at our antipodes,* he was already awake, having been prematurely roused either by the nocturnal visions that often put him in an agitated state as fatiguing as it is useless, or by the carnival that was then drawing to a closeâthis time of pleasure and folly having an influence on the human organism comparable to that of phases of the moon and the conjunction of certain planets.âIn short, he was awake, quite awake, in fact, when my soul also decided to shake off the bonds of sleep.
comment: Both translations are fine. There is no issue with the translation I did not select. The one I did select is just slightly less literal and therefore flows a little bit better in English.

source: Mais, le lendemain, il fut pris dâune peur dâenfant. Jamais il ne consentit Ã ce que les fenÃªtres fussent grandes ouvertes. Il murmurait : Â« Tout Ã lâheure, plus tard. Â» Il demeurait anxieux, il avait lâinquiÃ©tude du premier coup de lumiÃ¨re quâil recevrait dans les yeux. Le soir arriva, quâil nâavait pu prendre la dÃ©cision de revoir le soleil en face. Il Ã©tait restÃ© le visage tournÃ© vers les rideaux, suivant sur la transparence du linge le matin pÃ¢le, lâardent midi, le crÃ©puscule violÃ¢tre, toutes les couleurs, toutes les Ã©motions du ciel. LÃ , se peignait jusquâau frisson que le battement dâailes dâun oiseau donne Ã lâair tiÃ¨de, jusquâÃ?la joie des odeurs, palpitant dans un rayon. DerriÃ¨re ce voile, derriÃ¨re ce rÃªve attendri de la vie puissante du dehors, il Ã©coutait monter le printemps. Et mÃªme il Ã©touffait un peu, par moments, lorsque lâafflux du sang nouveau de la terre, malgrÃ© lâobstacle des rideaux, arrivait Ã lui trop rudement.
translation_a: But the next day he was seized by a childish fear. He would not allow the windows to be opened wide. â?In a while, later on,â?he would murmur. He remained anxious, worried about the effect of the first impact of light on his eyes. Evening came, and he still had not been able to make the decision to face the sun again. He had stayed with his face turned towards the curtains, following, on the transparence of the cloth, the pale morning light, the blaze of noon, the mauve of twilight, all the colours, all the emotions of the heavens. Upon that screen were reflected even the quivering of the warm air when a bird flaps its wings, and the joyfulness of the scents throbbing within a sunbeam. Behind that veil, that softened dream of the powerful life outside, he could hear spring arriving. And at times he was even suffocated, when in spite of the barrier of the curtains the rush of the earthâs new blood came at him too brutally.
translation_b: But the next day he was seized with the fear of a child. He never allowed the windows to be wide open. He whispered, âSoon, later. He remained anxious, he was worried about the first flash of light he would receive in his eyes. The evening arrived, that he had not been able to make the decision to see the sun again in front. He had remained with his face turned towards the curtains, following on the transparency of the linen the pale morning, the ardent noon, the violet twilight, all the colors, all the emotions of the sky. There, even the thrill that the flapping of a bird's wings gives to the warm air, even the joy of smells, quivering in a ray. Behind this veil, behind this tender dream of the powerful life outside, he listened to spring rising. And he even suffocated a little, at times, when the influx of new blood from the earth, despite the obstacle of the curtains, came to him too harshly.
comment: The translation I selected is by far the best as it is idiomatic and doesn't stick to French syntax. The other one is too literal and some of the sentences don't make sense in English. Take the French sentence "[...] la dÃ©cision de revoir le soleil en face". The expression "revoir ... en face" means, word by word, "to see again ... facing" which would normally be translated as "to face again". The GOOD translation got it right in "[...] the decision to face the sun again" while the BAD translation has it as "[...] the decision to see the sun again in front" which makes no sense in English.

source: Serge y vivait seul, dans une anxiÃ©tÃ© douloureuse, Ã©touffant davantage, couchant sur le canapÃ©, tÃ¢chant dâÃ©chapper aux soupirs du parc, aux odeurs des vieux meubles. La nuit, les nuditÃ©s des peintures lui donnaient des rÃªves fous, dont il ne gardait au rÃ©veil quâune inquiÃ©tude nerveuse. Il se crut malade de nouveau ; sa santÃ© avait un dernier besoin pour se rÃ©tablir complÃ¨tement, le besoin dâune plÃ©nitude suprÃªme, dâune satisfaction entiÃ¨re quâil ne savait oÃ¹ aller chercher. Alors, il passa ses journÃ©es, silencieux, les yeux meurtris, ne sâÃ©veillant dâun lÃ©ger tressaillement quâaux heures oÃ¹ Albine venait le voir. Ils demeuraient en face lâun de lâautre, Ã se regarder gravement, avec de rares paroles trÃ¨s douces, qui les navraient. Les yeux dâAlbine Ã©taient encore plus meurtris que ceux de Serge, et ils lâimploraient.
translation_a: Serge lived there alone, in painful anxiety, more suffocating, sleeping on the sofa, trying to escape the sighs of the park, the smells of old furniture. At night, the nudity of the paintings gave him mad dreams, of which he only kept a nervous anxiety when he woke up. He thought he was ill again; his health had one last need to recover completely, the need for a supreme plenitude, for an entire satisfaction which he did not know where to go to seek. So he passed his days, silent, his eyes bruised, waking with a slight start only at the hours when Albine came to see him. They remained facing each other, looking at each other gravely, with rare, very sweet words, which saddened them. Albine's eyes were even more bruised than Serge's, and they implored him.
translation_b: Serge lived there on his own, in a state of painful anxiety, suffocating even more, and sleeping on the sofa to try to avoid the sighs rising from the park and the odours of the old furniture. At night the naked figures in the paintings gave him mad dreams, of which all that remained when he awoke was a nervous uneasiness. He decided he had fallen ill again; his health needed one more thing to be fully recovered; that need was for a supreme fullness of being, an absolute satisfaction, and he did not know where it might be sought. So he spent the days in silence, with dark-ringed eyes, only coming awake with a slight start when Albine came to see him. They would stand, facing each other, with just a few tender words that made them even more unhappy. Albineâs eyes were even darker than Sergeâs, and seemed to beseech him.
comment: The translation I selected is by far the best. The other one is too literal and contains awkward phrasing. For example, compare the GOOD translation: "Serge lived there [...] suffocating even more" with the BAD translation: "Serge lived there [...] more suffocating". The part "more suffocating" sounds very weird in English. Additionally, I believe that some of the French was mistranslated. Take the French "Les yeux dâAlbine Ã©taient encore plus meurtris [...]" -- This clearly means that her eyes are showing signs of depression or something like that, and not that there is physical damage to her eyes. The GOOD translation has it as: "Albineâs eyes were even darker [...]" while the BAD translation has it as: "Albine's eyes were even more bruised [...]" I feel like this is saying that she got beaten up and got bruised eyes as a result. Even if you can use "bruised" metaphorically to mean "depressed", that would make the bad translation ambiguous. No need to add ambiguity when there isn't any in French.

source: Le jour revint, et il fut bien constatÃ© que, sous lâinfluence dâun courant sous-marin, le banc de glace dÃ©rivait vers le nord avec rapiditÃ©. Cette masse flottante emportait le Forward, clouÃ© au milieu de lâice-field, dont on ne voyait pas la limite ; dans la prÃ©vision dâune catastrophe, dans le cas oÃ¹ le brick serait jetÃ© sur une cÃ´te ou Ã©crasÃ© par la pression des glaces, Hatteras fit monter sur le pont une grande quantitÃ© de provisions, les effets de campement, les vÃªtements et les couvertures de lâÃ©quipage ; Ã lâexemple de ce que fit le capitaine MacClure dans une circonstance semblable, il fit entourer le bÃ¢timent dâune ceinture de hamacs gonflÃ©s dâair de maniÃ¨re Ã le prÃ©munir contre les grosses avaries ; bientÃ´t la glace, sâaccumulant sous lâinfluence dâune tempÃ©rature de sept degrÃ©s (-14Â° centigrades) ; le navire fut entourÃ© dâune muraille de laquelle sa mÃ¢ture sortait seule.
translation_a: The day returned, and it was clearly observed that, under the influence of an undercurrent, the ice bank was drifting rapidly towards the north. This floating mass carried the Forward, nailed to the middle of the ice-field, the limit of which could not be seen; in anticipation of a catastrophe, in case the brig should be thrown on a slope or crushed by the pressure of the ice, Hatteras brought on deck a large quantity of provisions, the camping effects, the clothes and blankets of the crew ; Following the example of what Captain MacClure did in a similar circumstance, he had the vessel surrounded by a belt of hammocks inflated with air so as to protect it against serious damage; soon the ice, accumulating under the influence of a temperature of seven degrees (-14Â° centigrade); the ship was surrounded by a wall from which its mast alone emerged.
translation_b: Day broke, and it was observed that the ice-field was floating north, in a strong submarine current. This floating mass transported the Forward, nailed to the heart of the field, without visible limit; foreseeing catastrophe if the brig was dashed on the coast or crushed by the ice, a large quantity of provisions, camping equipment, and blankets for the crew were brought on deck; like Captain McClure in similar circumstances, Hatteras had the ship protected with a ring of hammocks inflated with air, to prevent the worst damage; with soon a temperature of seven (â?4Â°C) and a build-up of ice, the ship was surrounded by a high wall, only its mast emerging.
comment: This was a difficult decision. For some reason, I am not a big fan of either translation. Both include great as well as poor phrasing. Ultimately, the reason why I chose the second translation is because the first one contains an error. In French, "[...] Hatteras fit monter sur le pont une grande quantitÃ© de provisions [...]" means that Hatteras HAD SOMEONE do something. He didn't do this thing himself. The translation I selected uses an avoidance strategy: "[...] a large quantity of provisions [...] were brought on deck" where the use of the passive erases agentivity (the person or thing performing the action of the verb). That works, because even though it is not a very accurate translation, it does not contradict the original meaning. The translation I did NOT select, however, did go into this contradiction with: "[...] Hatteras brought on deck a large quantity of provisions [...]". This sentence is a contradiction to the original text and it is false. It might seem like a detail, but it could potentially be of crucial importance for the story. You never know.

source: La vie du bord devint lâobjet de sÃ©rieuses mÃ©ditations ; Hatteras la rÃ©gla avec le plus grand soin, et le rÃ¨glement fut affichÃ© dans la salle commune. Les hommes se levaient Ã six heures du matin ; les hamacs Ã©taient exposÃ©s Ã lâair trois fois par semaine ; le plancher des deux chambres fut frottÃ© chaque matin avec du sable chaud ; le thÃ© brÃ»lant figurait Ã chaque repas, et la nourriture variait autant que possible suivant les jours de la semaine ; elle se composait de pain, de farine, de gras de bÅuf et de raisins secs pour les puddings, de sucre, de cacao, de thÃ©, de riz, de jus de citron, de viande conservÃ©e, de bÅuf et de porc salÃ©, de choux, et de lÃ©gumes au vinaigre ; la cuisine Ã©tait situÃ©e en dehors des salles communes ; on se privait ainsi de sa chaleur ; mais la cuisson des aliments est une source constante dâÃ©vaporation et dâhumiditÃ©.
translation_a: Life on board became the subject of serious consideration; Hatteras regulated it with the greatest attention, the guidelines being posted in the common room. The men got up at six; the hammocks were aired three times a week; the floors of both rooms were scrubbed with hot sand each morning; boiling tea accompanied each meal; the food changed as much as possible, depending on the day of the week: sugar, cocoa, tea, rice, lemon juice, preserved meat, beef, salt pork, cabbage, and pickled vegetables; plus puddings made from bread, flour, beef suet, and raisins; the kitchen was situated outside the common rooms; its heat was lost, but cooking is a constant source of evaporation and hence humidity.
translation_b: Life on board became the object of serious meditation; Hatteras settled it with the greatest care, and the rules were posted in the common room. The men got up at six o'clock in the morning; the hammocks were exposed to the air three times a week; the floor of the two rooms was scrubbed every morning with hot sand; hot tea figured at every meal, and the food varied as much as possible according to the days of the week; it consisted of bread, flour, beef fat and raisins for puddings, sugar, cocoa, tea, rice, lemon juice, preserved meat, salted beef and pork, cabbage, and pickled vegetables; the kitchen was located outside the common rooms; they thus deprived themselves of their warmth; but cooking food is a constant source of evaporation and moisture.
comment: The translation I selected is much more idiomatic and doesn't stick to French syntax. The other one is too literal and contains mistranslations. For example, the French verb "rÃ©gler" (line 1) has multiple meanings (to regulate, to settle, to adjust, to tune, etc.) The bad translation picked one of the meanings that is obviously not appropriate for the context. It picked the meaning of "settle" while the good translation picked the correct meaning of "regulate".

source: Elle le charmait par quantitÃ© de dÃ©licatesses : câÃ©tait tantÃ´t une maniÃ¨re nouvelle de faÃ§onner pour les bougies des bobÃ¨ches de papier, un volant quâelle changeait Ã sa robe, ou le nom extraordinaire dâun mets bien simple, et que la bonne avait manquÃ©, mais que Charles, jusquâau bout, avalait avec plaisir. Elle vit Ã Rouen des dames qui portaient Ã leur montre un paquet de breloques ; elle acheta des breloques. Elle voulut sur sa cheminÃ©e deux grands vases de verre bleu, et, quelque temps aprÃ¨s, un nÃ©cessaire dâivoire, avec un dÃ© de vermeil. Moins Charles comprenait ces Ã©lÃ©gances, plus il en subissait la sÃ©duction. Elles ajoutaient quelque chose au plaisir de ses sens et Ã la douceur de son foyer. CâÃ©tait comme une PoussiÃ¨re dâor qui sablait tout du long le petit sentier de sa vie.
translation_a: She charmed him with a number of delicacies: sometimes it was a new way of fashioning paper bobÃ¨ches for candles, a frill that she changed into her dress, or the extraordinary name of a very simple dish, which the maid had missed, but which Charles, right to the end, swallowed with pleasure. She saw ladies at Rouen who wore a bundle of trinkets on their watches; she bought charms. She wanted two large vases of blue glass on her mantelpiece, and, some time later, an ivory case with a silver-gilt thimble. The less Charles understood these elegances, the more he suffered from their seduction. They added something to the pleasure of his senses and to the comfort of his home. It was like a dust of gold that sanded all along the little path of his life.
translation_b: She would delight him with countless niceties; it was sometimes a new way of fashioning paper sconces for the candles, a flounce she would change on her dress, or the extraordinary name for a perfectly simple dish that the servant had spoiled, but every last bit of which Charles would swallow with pleasure. In Rouen she saw some ladies wearing clusters of charms on their watches; she bought some watch charms. She wanted two large vases of blue glass on her mantelpiece and, sometime after, an ivory sewing box, with a silver-gilt thimble. The less Charles understood these refinements, the more captivating he found them. They added something to the pleasure of his senses and to the sweetness of his home. They were like gold dust sprinkled all along the little path of his life.
comment: The translation I selected is by far the best because it is much more idiomatic and doesn't stick to French syntax. The other text was poorly translated, and in some places doesn't make sense. For example, in the French sentence "[...] un mets bien simple, et que la bonne avait manquÃ© [...]" - the verb "manquer" followed by a direct object can mean "to miss" as in "to miss the bus". However, in this context, it clearly means something else. It means that the servant had ruined / poorly cooked the dish. The author of the bad translation wrote that the servant had "missed the dish" which is a serious mistranslation.

translation_a: The stairs, on which the prince ran up from under the gate, led to the corridors of the first and second floors, along which the hotel rooms were located. This staircase, as in all houses built long ago, was of stone, dark, narrow, and wound around a thick stone pillar. On the first running platform in this column there was a recess, like a niche, no more than one step wide and half a step deep. A man, however, could fit here. Dark as it was, but, running up to the platform, the prince immediately discerned that here, in this niche, a man was hiding for some reason. The prince suddenly felt like passing by and not looking to the right. He took one step, but could not stand it and turned around.
translation_b: The stairs which the prince ran up from under the gateway led to the corridors of the first and second floors, on which the hotel rooms were located. This stairway, as in all houses built long ago, was of stone, dark, narrow, and winding around a thick stone pillar. On the first landing, this pillar turned out to have a depression in it, like a niche, no more than one pace wide and a half-pace deep. There was, however, room enough for a man. Having run up to the landing, the prince, despite the darkness, made out at once that a man was for some reason hiding there, in that niche. The prince suddenly wanted to walk past and not look to the right. He had already gone one step, but could not help himself and turned.
comment: Grammar is fine in both translations but I prefer the second one as the meaning is conveyed very precisely and close to the meaning of the Russian text. It is seen if we compare the sentence from the second translation "Having run up to the landing, the prince, despite the darkness, made out at once that a man was for some reason hiding there, in that niche" with a perfect vocabulary and structure to the sentence "Dark as it was, but, running up to the platform, the prince immediately discerned that here, in this niche, a man was hiding for some reason" from the first translation, which sounds machine. Vocabulary in the second translation is once again better: "landing" over "running platform", "could not help himself and turned" over "could not stand it and turned around".
